title: Cat Sudden Loss of Coordination: Emergency Guide & Next Steps
meta_desc: Is your cat stumbling or losing balance? Sudden loss of coordination is a high-priority emergency. Learn the causes and why you must act fast.
content_html:
What is Sudden Loss of Coordination?

Sudden loss of coordination, often referred to as ataxia, occurs when your cat loses the ability to control their body movements normally. You may notice your cat stumbling, swaying, falling over, or appearing 'drunk' while walking. This condition is usually caused by a disruption in the nervous system, the inner ear (vestibular system), or the brain, affecting their sense of space and balance.

Is This a Veterinary Emergency?

Urgency Level: High. Yes, the sudden onset of balance issues is a medical emergency. Because these symptoms can be caused by serious underlying conditions such as strokes, neurological trauma, toxin ingestion, or severe infections, your cat needs to be evaluated by a veterinarian immediately. Prompt intervention is critical to ensure the best possible outcome for your pet.

Why a Photo or Video Helps Triage

When you are preparing to head to the clinic, capturing a short video of your cat's behavior can be an invaluable tool for the veterinary team. Cats often hide their symptoms due to the stress of travel or a new environment. A video allows the vet to see exactly how your cat was moving at home, helping them distinguish between different types of ataxia or identifying involuntary eye movements (nystagmus) that may have subsided by the time you arrive.

faqs.question: Can an ear infection cause my cat to lose its balance?
faqs.answer: Yes, a deep inner ear infection can affect the vestibular system, which is responsible for balance, leading to stumbling and a head tilt.

faqs.question: Is it possible my cat had a stroke?
faqs.answer: While less common in cats than in humans, strokes or vascular events can occur and often manifest as a sudden loss of coordination or weakness on one side of the body.

faqs.question: How should I transport my cat to the emergency vet?
faqs.answer: Place your cat in a secure, well-padded carrier to prevent them from falling or injuring themselves further during the car ride.
